stem: In Claude Code, you need to find every file that CONTAINS the string `processRefund`. Which tool?
options: Glob — it searches file patterns
Grep — it searches file contents; Glob is for finding files by name/path pattern
Read — open likely files and scan them
Bash with `find`
rationales: Glob matches filenames and paths, not what's inside files.
Grep for text inside files, Glob for names and paths — using filename search to find code references is the classic mixup.
Reading files one by one is exploration by brute force.
`find` locates files by name; it doesn't search contents (that's what Grep wraps).

stem: Which task calls for plan mode rather than direct execution?
options: Fixing a typo in one README line
Renaming a local variable in one function
Adding a log statement to debug one code path
A migration spanning many files with architectural choices that stakeholders must approve before edits
rationales: Tiny, low-risk, obvious target — plan mode is pure overhead here.
Same: small, localized, clear target.
Low-risk and easily reversed.
Multi-file scope, architecture decisions, approval gates, and read-only exploration first — every plan mode trigger at once. Plan mode gates the transition from thinking to doing.

stem: The agent keeps jumping straight to edits without surfacing trade-offs. Separately, its analyses of a gnarly concurrency bug feel shallow. Which mechanism addresses each?
options: Plan mode for the edit-gating problem (workflow control); extended thinking / higher effort for the shallow analysis (reasoning quality) — they solve different problems and can combine
Extended thinking for both — more reasoning fixes everything
Plan mode for both — planning forces deeper analysis
Neither; both require a larger model
rationales: Plan mode gates when actions happen and inserts human review; effort scales how hard the model reasons. One is workflow, one is cognition.
Thinking harder doesn't add an approval gate before edits.
Plan mode doesn't deepen reasoning on a hard problem — it sequences the workflow.
Both problems have dedicated mechanisms on the current model.

stem: You want to evaluate two alternative implementations of a feature, starting from the same session state, without the attempts contaminating each other. What is the right setup?
options: Resume the original session twice in two terminals
Copy-paste the context into two fresh sessions
`--fork-session` for each approach so transcripts diverge independently, AND separate git worktrees so the file changes don't collide — sessions persist conversation, not filesystem state
One session, alternating between the approaches
rationales: Concurrent resumes of one session append to the same history and confuse later resumes.
Pasting loses tool-call history the fork preserves.
Fork isolates the conversations; worktrees isolate the files. Forking without file isolation leaves both editing one checkout; file isolation without forking tangles the transcripts.
Interleaved attempts in one transcript is exactly the contamination to avoid.

stem: You've worked on several unrelated tasks in this directory. You want to return to a specific investigation from last week. Which flag?
options: `--continue` — it resumes your work
`--resume` — it lets you pick the specific session; `--continue` blindly grabs the most recent conversation, which may be the wrong one
`--session-id` with a new UUID
No flag — Claude Code auto-detects the intended session
rationales: `--continue` takes the latest session without prompting — in a multi-task directory, "latest" is often not "the one you meant."
`--resume` opens a picker (or takes an identifier) for exactly this case: returning to a known, specific session.
A new UUID creates a fresh session — the opposite of resuming.
There is no intent detection; you choose.

stem: Why is the session that wrote the code often the wrong place to review it, and what is the alternative?
options: Long sessions run out of tokens for review
Reviews require different API permissions
The writing session has no access to the diff
Its context includes the earlier reasoning, biasing it toward its own choices — use a fresh review context, a dedicated review subagent, CI review, or a separate session given the diff and criteria
rationales: Token budget isn't the mechanism — bias is.
No permission difference exists.
It has full access — that's part of the problem.
Context isolation is the point: a reviewer that didn't watch the code being justified judges it on its merits.

stem: A team writes "NEVER run destructive Bash commands without approval" in CLAUDE.md and considers the risk handled. What is the correct assessment?
options: CLAUDE.md is context, not enforcement — the model tries to follow it with no compliance guarantee; a `PreToolUse` hook or `permissions.deny` is the mechanism for must-always-hold rules
Handled — CLAUDE.md instructions are binding configuration
Handled if the same rule also appears in auto memory
Handled if the rule is written in all caps
rationales: Memory shapes behavior; hooks enforce it. Hooks run as code in your environment and cannot be talked around — which is exactly why hard rules belong there.
Nothing in CLAUDE.md is enforced; it's soft guidance the model reads.
Auto memory is also context — duplicating guidance doesn't create enforcement.
Capitalization aids salience, not compliance.

stem: Which statement about CLAUDE.md loading is TRUE?
options: All CLAUDE.md files in the entire repository load at launch
Only the single closest CLAUDE.md loads

Working-directory and ancestor CLAUDE.md files load fully at launch; subdirectory ones load on demand when files in those subtrees are read; `@imports` expand at launch, so they organize content but don't save tokens
Imports load lazily, so splitting CLAUDE.md via @imports reduces context cost
rationales: Subtree files below the working directory wait until relevant files are read.
The whole ancestor chain loads, broader scopes first.
Three loading behaviors, one system: eager ancestors, lazy subdirectories, eagerly-expanded imports. The token-saving mechanism is path-scoped rules, not imports.
That's the trap — imports expand into context at launch.

stem: In `.claude/rules/`, what is the difference between a rule file WITH `paths:` frontmatter and one without?
options: Without paths the rule is disabled
Without `paths:` the rule loads unconditionally every session (like `.claude/CLAUDE.md`); with `paths:` it enters context only when Claude reads files matching the globs
`paths:` controls which users receive the rule
`paths:` rules override CLAUDE.md; unscoped rules don't
rationales: No-paths rules are fully active — just always-on.
That's the scoping mechanism: area-specific conventions cost zero context until the area is actually touched.
Paths scope by file location, not by person.
Priority isn't the axis; loading condition is.

stem: Which statement about Claude Code's auto memory is TRUE?
options: It syncs across all your machines via your account
The entire memory directory loads at every session start
It is enforced configuration, unlike CLAUDE.md
The first 200 lines or 25KB of MEMORY.md load at session start; topic files load on demand; it is machine-local and shared across worktrees of the same repo
rationales: Auto memory does not sync across machines or cloud environments.
Only the MEMORY.md head loads eagerly — topic files wait until read.
It's context Claude maintains for itself — soft guidance like all memory.
Those are the operative limits and behaviors — including that all worktrees of one repository share a single memory directory.

stem: When is a SKILL the right mechanism instead of a slash command?
options: When the workflow should trigger from the nature of the task — Claude recognizes from the description that it applies (e.g., "this is a database migration") — while slash commands stay deliberate human acts
When the workflow needs more than ten steps
When the workflow must be enforced deterministically
Never — skills are deprecated in favor of slash commands
rationales: The dividing line is who initiates: skills can self-trigger from task recognition (and load their full body on demand — a 400-line checklist costs one description line until needed); slash commands run only when a human types them.
Length doesn't choose the mechanism.
Neither skills nor commands enforce — that's hooks.
Both are current, complementary mechanisms.

stem: Claude keeps ignoring a project convention you're sure you wrote down. What is the FIRST diagnostic step?
options: Rewrite the rule more forcefully with IMPORTANT markers
Duplicate the rule into every CLAUDE.md scope

Run `/memory` to confirm the file containing the rule is actually loaded for this working directory — if it isn't loading, no wording change will help; for lazy-loading rules, the `InstructionsLoaded` hook shows exactly what loaded and why
Disable auto memory in case it conflicts
rationales: Rewording a rule that never loads accomplishes nothing.
Duplication bloats every session and still doesn't diagnose the actual failure.
Check loading before wording: `/memory` lists every loaded CLAUDE.md, local file, and rule. Most "Claude ignores my rules" cases are loading-scope problems.
Auto memory rarely conflicts, and disabling it is a shot in the dark.

stem: Before enabling a hook configuration from a third-party source, what should you do?
options: Nothing — hooks run in a sandbox
Review it like any code with security implications — hooks execute in your environment and a malicious or buggy hook can damage your system or exfiltrate data
Test it with a read-only API key first
Enable it only for PostToolUse events, which are safe
rationales: Hooks are NOT sandboxed — they run as shell commands (or SDK callbacks) in your environment.
The power that makes hooks the right enforcement layer also makes them a supply-chain risk. Review before enabling; keep secrets out of arguments hooks might log.
API keys don't limit what a shell command can do to your filesystem.
PostToolUse hooks are just as much arbitrary code as any other.
